events.title: Cyrus the Great Founds Achaemenid Empire
events.detail: Cyrus II defeats the Median king Astyages at the Battle of Pasargadae and unites the Medes and Persians under a single crown, founding history's first superpower. Within a decade he will conquer Lydia (547 BCE) and Babylon (539 BCE), ruling from the Aegean to Central Asia. His administration of subject peoples through regional governors (satraps) becomes a model for future empires.

sources.name: Herodotus, Histories (I.95–130)
Pierre Briant, 'From Cyrus to Alexander' (Eisenbrauns, 2002)

events.title: Cyrus Conquers Babylon — Issues the Cyrus Cylinder
events.detail: Cyrus enters Babylon without a battle, positioning himself as liberator rather than conqueror. He issues the Cyrus Cylinder — a clay baked declaration in Akkadian cuneiform announcing religious tolerance, the right of deported peoples to return home (including Jews), and restoration of sacred sanctuaries. Often called the world's first human rights charter, though historians debate this characterization.

sources.name: The Cyrus Cylinder, British Museum (BM 90920)
Irving Finkel, 'The Cyrus Cylinder' (2013, I.B. Tauris)

events.title: Darius I Consolidates Power — Behistun Inscription
events.detail: After a series of revolts following Cambyses II's death, Darius I seizes the throne and spends years crushing nineteen rebel kings across the empire. He commissions the monumental Behistun Inscription on a cliff in western Iran — carved in Old Persian, Elamite, and Babylonian — recording his victories and legitimacy. Darius reorganizes the empire into 20 satrapies with standardized coinage (the daric), royal roads, and the Nowruz (Persian New Year) festival system.

sources.name: Behistun Inscription (primary source, c. 520 BCE)
A. T. Olmstead, 'History of the Persian Empire' (University of Chicago Press, 1948)

events.title: Battle of Marathon — First Greco-Persian War
events.detail: Darius I dispatches an expeditionary force to punish Athens and Eretria for their support of the Ionian Revolt. Despite landing at Marathon with a numerically superior force, the Persian army is defeated by Athenian hoplites under Miltiades. The victory halts Persian expansion into mainland Greece and becomes a foundational myth of Western democracy, though Persia remains the dominant world power.

sources.name: Herodotus, Histories (VI.112–117)
Peter Green, 'The Greco-Persian Wars' (University of California Press, 1996)

events.title: Xerxes I Invades Greece — Thermopylae and Salamis
events.detail: Xerxes leads the largest army of the ancient world — ancient sources estimate 1–2 million, modern historians suggest 100,000–300,000 — across the Hellespont via a pontoon bridge. He defeats the Spartans under Leonidas at Thermopylae, burns Athens, but suffers a catastrophic naval defeat at Salamis when his fleet is lured into narrow straits by the Athenian strategos Themistocles. The Battle of Plataea (479 BCE) seals Greek independence. Despite the failure, Persia remains the superpower for another 150 years.

sources.name: Herodotus, Histories (VII–IX)
Tom Holland, 'Persian Fire' (Doubleday, 2005)

events.title: Construction of Persepolis Begins
events.detail: Darius I initiates construction of Persepolis (Parsa), the ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Empire, on a vast terrace carved into the mountain at the foot of Kuh-e Rahmat in Fars province. The complex — including the Apadana audience hall, the Gate of All Nations, and the Treasury — took over 60 years to complete. Every spring, delegations from all 23 subject nations brought tribute and gifts for Nowruz (New Year). Alexander the Great burned Persepolis in 330 BCE, reportedly in revenge for the burning of Athens, but the ruins have survived to the present day.

sources.name: Persepolis Fortification Tablets (Oriental Institute, Chicago)
UNESCO World Heritage List #114 (1979)

events.title: Alexander the Great Defeats Darius III — End of Achaemenid Empire
events.detail: Alexander of Macedon defeats the last Achaemenid emperor Darius III at Gaugamela (331 BCE), then seizes Persepolis and its treasury. He burns the city — possibly in a deliberate act of vengeance or during a drunken celebration. Darius III is later murdered by his own satrap Bessus. The Achaemenid Empire, which had ruled for 220 years and covered 5.5 million sq km, falls. Alexander incorporates Persian administrative structures and customs, beginning two centuries of Hellenistic influence in the region.

sources.name: Arrian, 'Anabasis of Alexander' (c. 150 CE)
Peter Green, 'Alexander of Macedon' (University of California Press, 1991)

events.title: Arsaces I Founds the Parthian Empire
events.detail: Arsaces I, leader of the Parni nomadic tribe, rebels against the Seleucid satrap of Parthia and founds the Arsacid dynasty. The Parthians gradually expand westward, eventually controlling all of Iran and Mesopotamia. Their decentralized feudal system, heavily armored cavalry (cataphracts), and skilled mounted archers make them Rome's most formidable eastern rival for 400 years. The Parthian capital Ctesiphon (near modern Baghdad) becomes a major commercial hub on the Silk Road.

sources.name: Strabo, 'Geography' (XI.9.3)
Vesta Sarkhosh Curtis, 'The Parthian Empire' (British Museum, 2012)

events.title: Battle of Carrhae — Parthians Destroy Roman Army
events.detail: Roman triumvir Marcus Licinius Crassus invades Parthia with 40,000 legionaries seeking glory to match Caesar and Pompey. Parthian general Surena lures the Romans into the Syrian desert near Carrhae (modern Harran, Turkey) and destroys the army with horse archers and cataphracts. Crassus is killed. Rome loses 20,000 dead and 10,000 captured — the eagles of seven legions are taken. The defeat shocks Rome and demonstrates that Parthia, not Rome, controls the eastern world.

sources.name: Plutarch, 'Life of Crassus' (c. 100 CE)
Touraj Daryaee, 'The Oxford Handbook of Iranian History' (OUP, 2012)

events.title: Ardashir I Founds Sassanid Empire — Persian Restoration
events.detail: Ardashir I, a local Persian king from Istakhr (near Persepolis), defeats and kills the last Parthian king Artabanus IV at the Battle of Hormozdgan. He proclaims himself 'King of Kings of Iran' (Shahanshah) and founds the Sassanid dynasty, consciously reviving Achaemenid traditions. Zoroastrianism becomes the state religion. The Sassanids rule for over 400 years, engaging in constant wars with Rome (later Byzantium) and eventually conquering Egypt (619 CE) before their sudden collapse before Arab armies.

sources.name: The Ka'ba-ye Zartosht inscription (primary source, c. 260 CE)
Touraj Daryaee, 'Sasanian Persia' (I.B. Tauris, 2009)

events.title: Shapur I Captures Roman Emperor Valerian at Edessa
events.detail: Shapur I defeats and personally captures Roman Emperor Valerian at the Battle of Edessa — the only time in Roman history that a sitting emperor was taken prisoner by a foreign enemy. Shapur commemorates the victory with rock reliefs at Naqsh-e Rostam and Bishapur showing Valerian kneeling before him. The 70,000 Roman prisoners are used as skilled laborers to build cities and infrastructure including the Band-e Kaisar dam in Khuzestan.

sources.name: Shapur's Res Gestae inscription, Ka'ba-ye Zartosht (c. 262 CE)
Historia Augusta (c. 4th century CE)

events.title: Khosrow I Anushirvan — Sassanid Golden Age
events.detail: Khosrow I ('Immortal Soul') reigns for 48 years, reforming taxation, strengthening the military, and patronizing learning. He invites Greek philosophers expelled by Justinian from Athens, expanding the Academy of Gondishapur into a renowned center of medicine, philosophy, and astronomy. He defeats the Byzantine Emperor Justinian and extracts a 'perpetual peace' payment. His reign is remembered as a golden age of just governance — the ideal of the just Persian king that would influence Islamic political thought for centuries.

sources.name: Agathias of Myrina, 'Histories' (c. 580 CE)
Ehsan Yarshater (ed.), 'Cambridge History of Iran Vol. 3' (CUP, 1983)

events.title: Arab Muslim Conquest — Battle of al-Qadisiyyah
events.detail: Arab Muslim forces under Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas defeat the Sassanid army at al-Qadisiyyah (near modern Najaf, Iraq) after three days of battle. The decisive victory opens Mesopotamia to Arab conquest. Ctesiphon falls; the last Sassanid emperor Yazdegerd III flees eastward and is murdered in 651 CE. Iran is gradually incorporated into the Islamic Caliphate over the following decades, ending nearly 1,300 years of Iranian-ruled empires. The conquest transforms Iranian culture, language, and religion — though Persia ultimately transforms Arabic-Islamic civilization as much as it is transformed by it.

sources.name: al-Tabari, 'History of the Prophets and Kings' (9th century CE)
Richard Frye, 'The Golden Age of Persia' (Weidenfeld & Nicolson, 1975)

events.title: Abbasid Revolution — Persian Influence Reshapes Islam
events.detail: Persian mawali (clients/converts) and the Khorasanian army under Abu Muslim Khorasani help the Abbasids overthrow the Umayyad Caliphate. The Abbasid capital shifts to Baghdad (founded 762 CE), and Persian administrative culture, court etiquette, and intellectual traditions become dominant. The Persian concept of kingship, bureaucratic expertise, and Persian-language scholarship shape early Islamic civilization — a process historians call the 'Iranian Intermezzo' in Islamic history.

sources.name: Hugh Kennedy, 'The Prophet and the Age of the Caliphates' (Longman, 2004)
al-Tabari, 'History' (c. 915 CE)

events.title: Samanid Dynasty — Persian Cultural Renaissance
events.detail: The Samanid dynasty, ruling from Bukhara (in modern Uzbekistan) and Khorasan, becomes the first independent Persian-ruled state after the Islamic conquest. Samanid rulers patronize Persian-language literature, replacing Arabic as the court language. Rudaki (the 'Father of Persian Poetry'), al-Biruni, and Avicenna (Ibn Sina) flourish in this environment. The Persian language, written in Arabic script as Dari/Farsi, undergoes a renaissance that would define Iranian cultural identity for the next millennium.

sources.name: Richard Frye, 'The Heritage of Persia' (Weidenfeld & Nicolson, 1963)
Encyclopaedia Iranica, 'Samanids' entry (Columbia University)

events.title: Ferdowsi Completes the Shahnameh (Book of Kings)
events.detail: Abu'l-Qasem Ferdowsi presents his 60,000-couplet Shahnameh to Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni — the most celebrated work of Persian literature. The epic narrates the mythological and historical kings of Iran from creation to the Arab conquest, preserving pre-Islamic Persian traditions and heroic narratives (Rostam and Sohrab, the tragic Siyavash, etc.) in pure Persian largely devoid of Arabic loanwords. The Shahnameh becomes the cornerstone of Iranian national identity and is recited in Iranian households to this day.

sources.name: Ferdowsi, 'Shahnameh' (c. 977–1010 CE)
Dick Davis (trans.), 'Shahnameh: The Persian Book of Kings' (Viking, 2006)

events.title: Omar Khayyam — Mathematics, Astronomy & the Rubaiyat
events.detail: Born in Nishapur, Omar Khayyam reforms the Persian solar calendar (the Jalali calendar, 1079 CE) for Seljuk Sultan Malik-Shah — more accurate than the Gregorian calendar adopted in Europe 500 years later. His mathematical treatise on cubic equations predates European algebra by centuries. His Rubaiyat quatrains, translated by Edward FitzGerald in 1859, introduce Persian poetry to the Western world and become among the most-read English-language poems. Khayyam epitomizes the Iran-Islamic scientific golden age.

sources.name: Omar Khayyam, 'Risala fi sharh ma ashkala min musadarat kitab Uqlidis' (c. 1077)
Encyclopaedia Iranica, 'Omar Khayyam' (Columbia University)

events.title: Mongol Invasion — Catastrophic Destruction of Iranian Cities
events.detail: Genghis Khan's Mongol armies invade the Khwarazmian Empire (which spans modern Iran) after Shah Ala ad-Din Mohammad II executes Mongol trade envoys. Nishapur, Merv, Herat, Balkh, and Ray are sacked with systematic mass killings. Merv, one of the world's largest cities, is virtually depopulated. Estimates of deaths across the invasion range from 10 to 40 million across Central Asia and Iran — Iran's population may have fallen by 50–75% in the worst-affected regions. The agricultural infrastructure of Khorasan is devastated and takes centuries to recover.

sources.name: Ata-Malik Juvayni, 'History of the World Conqueror' (c. 1260 CE)
David Morgan, 'The Mongols' (Blackwell, 2007)

events.title: Hulagu Khan Destroys Baghdad — Ilkhanate Established
events.detail: Hulagu Khan, grandson of Genghis, sacks and destroys Baghdad, killing the last Abbasid Caliph al-Musta'sim. The House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) and its libraries are destroyed — an incalculable loss to human knowledge. The Ilkhanate (Mongol-ruled Persia) eventually converts to Islam and patronizes Persian art and culture, producing some of Iran's greatest illuminated manuscripts and the Ilkhanid architectural style. Ilkhanid Persia becomes a crucial bridge between East Asian and Islamic artistic traditions.

sources.name: Rashid al-Din Hamadani, 'Compendium of Chronicles' (c. 1307 CE)
Reuven Amitai-Preiss, 'Mongols and Mamluks' (CUP, 1995)

events.title: Timur (Tamerlane) Conquers Persia — Second Wave of Destruction
events.detail: Timur (Tamerlane), a Turco-Mongol warlord from Samarkand, launches devastating campaigns across Persia, leaving towers of skulls outside captured cities. Isfahan's population of 200,000 is massacred after resistance (1387). Despite the destruction, Timur patronizes art and architecture — Samarkand becomes one of the world's great cities. His Timurid successors (15th century) make Herat a center of Persian miniature painting. The Timurid cultural renaissance directly influences the Safavids and the Mughal Empire of India.

sources.name: Ruy González de Clavijo, 'Embassy to Tamerlane' (1406)
Beatrice Forbes Manz, 'The Rise and Rule of Tamerlane' (CUP, 1989)

events.title: Shah Ismail I Founds Safavid Dynasty — Iran Becomes Shia State
events.detail: Ismail I, teenage leader of the Safavid Sufi order, conquers Tabriz and declares Twelver Shia Islam the official state religion of Iran — a geopolitical revolution that defines Iranian identity to this day. Ismail forcibly converts the largely Sunni population, importing Shia clerics from Iraq and Lebanon. The Safavid state provides both a buffer against Sunni Ottoman expansion and the institutional foundation for the Shia clergy (ulama) that will eventually produce the Islamic Republic's governing ideology of velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the jurist).

sources.name: Roger Savory, 'Iran Under the Safavids' (CUP, 1980)
IRNA — Official Iranian historical record

events.title: Battle of Chaldiran — Ottoman Victory Shapes Modern Borders
events.detail: Ottoman Sultan Selim I defeats Safavid Shah Ismail I at Chaldiran in eastern Anatolia, using artillery that the Safavids lack. Ismail is wounded and never fights again personally; his aura of invincibility (as a divine figure) is shattered. The battle stabilizes the Ottoman-Safavid border roughly along modern Turkey-Iran lines, fixes the Sunni-Shia sectarian divide in the region, and leaves the Safavids permanently on the defensive against the Ottomans in the west while they focus on consolidating Iran and fighting the Uzbeks in the east.

sources.name: Andrew Newman, 'Safavid Iran: Rebirth of a Persian Empire' (I.B. Tauris, 2006)
John Woods, 'The Aqquyunlu: Clan, Confederation, Empire' (University of Utah Press, 1999)

events.title: Shah Abbas I — Safavid Golden Age, Isfahan as World Capital
events.detail: Shah Abbas I ('the Great') transforms Iran into a regional superpower. He moves the capital to Isfahan, building the Imam (Shah) Mosque, Ali Qapu Palace, and Naqsh-e Jahan Square — among the most magnificent architectural ensembles in the world. He defeats the Uzbeks and reconquers Khorasan from the Ottomans using a reformed army with European muskets. Abbas expels the Portuguese from Hormuz (1622) with English help. Isfahan's population reaches 500,000, comparable to London; European travelers call it 'half the world' (Isfahan nesf-e jahan ast).

sources.name: Jean Chardin, 'Travels in Persia' (1686, English trans. 1927)
David Blow, 'Shah Abbas: The Ruthless King Who Became an Iranian Legend' (I.B. Tauris, 2009)

events.title: Treaty of Zuhab — Ottoman-Persian Border Fixed
events.detail: After over a century of warfare, the Ottoman Empire and Safavid Persia sign the Treaty of Zuhab (also known as the Treaty of Qasr-e Shirin), fixing borders that roughly correspond to the modern Iraq-Iran frontier. The treaty recognizes Mesopotamia (Iraq) as Ottoman and Persia as Safavid, separating Arabic-speaking Shia populations from Persian Shia governance — a division with profound consequences for modern Middle Eastern politics, including the Iran-Iraq War of 1980–88.

sources.name: Rudi Matthee, 'Persia in Crisis: Safavid Decline and the Fall of Isfahan' (I.B. Tauris, 2012)
Cambridge History of Iran Vol. 6 (CUP, 1986)

events.title: Afghan Invasion Ends Safavid Rule
events.detail: Mir Mahmud Hotaki leads a Ghilzai Afghan force against the weakening Safavid empire, defeating Shah Sultan Husayn at the Battle of Gulnabad and besieging Isfahan for six months, causing mass starvation. The Shah surrenders and the Safavid dynasty collapses. Russia (Peter the Great) and the Ottomans opportunistically seize Iranian territory in the north and west. The interregnum creates chaos until the rise of Nader Shah, but the Safavid legacy of Shia statehood permanently defines Iranian national identity.

sources.name: Laurence Lockhart, 'The Fall of the Safavid Dynasty' (CUP, 1958)
Michael Axworthy, 'Empire of the Mind: A History of Iran' (Hurst, 2007)

events.title: Nader Shah — Military Genius Crowns Himself, Invades India
events.detail: Nader Shah Afshar, a military general from Khorasan, expels the Afghans, defeats the Ottomans and Russians, and crowns himself Shah of Iran at Mughan plain. He invades Mughal India (1739), defeating the Emperor Muhammad Shah and sacking Delhi — bringing back the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond. His empire briefly stretches from the Caucasus to northwestern India. Nader attempts to reconcile Sunni and Shia Islam, but is assassinated in 1747. Despite his brilliance, his heavy taxation devastates Iran's economy.

sources.name: Michael Axworthy, 'The Sword of Persia: Nader Shah' (I.B. Tauris, 2006)
Encyclopaedia Iranica, 'Nader Shah' (Columbia University)

events.title: Qajar Dynasty Established — Tehran Becomes Capital
events.detail: Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar reunifies Iran after decades of civil war, crowning himself Shah and establishing Tehran — a small village near the ruins of Ray — as the new capital. He systematically destroys rivals, including massacring the population of Tbilisi. The Qajar dynasty rules until 1925, presiding over Iran's transformation from a great regional power to a semi-colonized state progressively losing territory and sovereignty to British and Russian imperial pressure (the 'Great Game').

sources.name: Abbas Amanat, 'Iran: A Modern History' (Yale University Press, 2017)
John Foran, 'Fragile Resistance: Social Transformation in Iran' (Westview Press, 1993)

events.title: Treaty of Gulistan — Iran Cedes Caucasus to Russia
events.detail: Iran signs the Treaty of Gulistan with Russia following defeat in the Russo-Persian War, ceding Dagestan, Georgia, most of Azerbaijan, and other Caucasian territories. The Second Russo-Persian War (1826–28) results in the Treaty of Turkmenchay (1828), further ceding Armenia and giving Russia extraterritorial rights in Iran. These humiliating defeats at Russian hands — mirrored by British encroachments in the east — create a deep Persian sense of national humiliation and victimhood at the hands of foreign powers that shapes Iranian politics to the present day.

sources.name: Firuz Kazemzadeh, 'Russia and Britain in Persia' (Yale UP, 1968)
Nikki Keddie, 'Qajar Iran and the Rise of Reza Khan' (Mazda Press, 1999)

events.title: Constitutional Revolution — Iran's First Parliament (Majlis)
events.detail: Mass protests led by merchants, clerics, and intellectuals force Mozaffar ad-Din Shah to sign a constitution and establish the Majlis (parliament) — one of the first constitutional movements in Asia. The revolution reflects Iranians' desire for rule of law, limits on royal power, and national sovereignty against British and Russian influence. However, British and Russian intervention (1907 Anglo-Russian Convention dividing Iran into spheres of influence), combined with internal conflict between liberal nationalists and conservative clerics, prevents the revolution from achieving stable democratic governance.

sources.name: Janet Afary, 'The Iranian Constitutional Revolution, 1906–1911' (Columbia UP, 1996)
Ervand Abrahamian, 'Iran Between Two Revolutions' (Princeton UP, 1982)

events.title: First Commercial Oil Discovery — Masjed Soleyman Well No. 1
events.detail: British engineer George Reynolds strikes oil at Masjed Soleyman in Khuzestan (May 26, 1908) — the first major commercial oil discovery in the Middle East. The Anglo-Persian Oil Company (APOC, later BP) is formed to exploit the concession granted under the 1901 D'Arcy Concession. By 1914, Winston Churchill, as First Lord of the Admiralty, converts the Royal Navy to oil, giving British control of Iranian oil critical strategic importance. Iran receives only a small royalty from the profits — a source of growing national resentment.

sources.name: Daniel Yergin, 'The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil' (Simon & Schuster, 1991)
BP Corporate Archive — D'Arcy Concession documents (1901)

events.title: Reza Khan's Coup — British-Backed Military Rise to Power
events.detail: Reza Khan, commander of the Cossack Brigade (a Russian-trained force now under British direction), leads a coup from Qazvin to Tehran, establishing military control over the government. British general Ironside is widely believed to have encouraged the coup. Reza Khan serves as War Minister then Prime Minister before deposing the last Qajar Shah and crowning himself Reza Shah Pahlavi in 1925. His rule modernizes Iran's infrastructure, military, and administration while suppressing tribal and regional autonomy — a model explicitly inspired by Atatürk's Turkey.

sources.name: Cyrus Ghani, 'Iran and the Rise of Reza Shah' (I.B. Tauris, 1998)
Ervand Abrahamian, 'A History of Modern Iran' (CUP, 2008)

events.title: Reza Khan Crowned Reza Shah — Pahlavi Dynasty Founded
events.detail: Reza Khan deposes Ahmad Shah Qajar and crowns himself Reza Shah Pahlavi, adopting the name 'Pahlavi' (after the Middle Persian language) to evoke ancient Persian heritage. He launches an aggressive modernization program: builds the Trans-Iranian Railway, founds Tehran University (1934), emancipates women from the veil (1936), centralizes governance, and creates a modern conscript army. He renames 'Persia' to 'Iran' in international usage (1935). His authoritarian nationalism suppresses ethnic minorities and political opposition while transforming Iran's physical and institutional landscape.

sources.name: Stephanie Cronin, 'The Army and the Creation of the Pahlavi State in Iran' (Tauris Academic, 1997)
Homa Katouzian, 'The Political Economy of Modern Iran' (Macmillan, 1981)

events.title: Allied Invasion — Reza Shah Abdicates
events.detail: Britain and the Soviet Union jointly invade Iran (Operation Countenance, August 25, 1941) to prevent a feared German-friendly Reza Shah from cutting off supply routes to the Soviet Union. Despite the Shah's declaration of neutrality, the Allies depose him in three days and exile him to South Africa, where he dies in 1944. His 22-year-old son Mohammad Reza is installed as Shah — a compliant constitutional monarch. Iran becomes the 'Persian Corridor' supplying the Soviets with 40% of their Lend-Lease supplies; over 120,000 Allied troops occupy the country until 1946.

sources.name: T.H. Vail Motter, 'The Persian Corridor and Aid to Russia' (US Army WWII history series, 1952)
Ervand Abrahamian, 'A History of Modern Iran' (CUP, 2008)

events.title: Mosaddegh Nationalizes Anglo-Iranian Oil — National Hero
events.detail: Prime Minister Mohammad Mosaddegh's government passes the Oil Nationalization Act (May 1, 1951), taking the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company (AIOC) into Iranian state ownership. Iran had been receiving only 16% of oil profits under existing agreements. The nationalization triggers a British-led economic blockade and a legal battle at the International Court of Justice (which Iran wins on procedural grounds in 1952). Mosaddegh wins Time magazine's Person of the Year (1951). The nationalization makes him a global symbol of anti-colonialism.

sources.name: Homa Katouzian, 'Mosaddegh and the Struggle for Power in Iran' (I.B. Tauris, 1990)
ICJ, 'Anglo-Iranian Oil Co. Case' (1952)

events.title: CIA/MI6 Coup Overthrows Mosaddegh (Operation Ajax/Boot)
events.detail: The CIA (Operation Ajax) and British MI6 (Operation Boot) orchestrate a coup overthrowing Prime Minister Mosaddegh on August 19, 1953. CIA officer Kermit Roosevelt Jr. coordinates paid mobs, military officers, and clerics to create the appearance of popular revolt. Mosaddegh is arrested, tried for treason, and sentenced to house arrest until his death in 1967. The Shah returns from exile in Rome. The US and UK restore the Shah's power in exchange for an oil consortium giving them a 40% share. The coup's memory becomes the central Iranian grievance against Western interference.

sources.name: Declassified CIA documents — 'The Battle for Iran' (released 2013)
Stephen Kinzer, 'All the Shah's Men' (Wiley, 2003)
IRNA / Iranian government historical record

events.title: White Revolution — Shah's Modernization Reforms
events.detail: Mohammad Reza Shah launches the 'White Revolution' (Revolution of the Shah and People), a six-point reform program ratified by referendum that includes land reform breaking up large estates, women's suffrage and eligibility for election (a first in the Muslim world), nationalization of forests, profit-sharing for industrial workers, literacy corps, and sale of state factories. The reforms weaken the traditional landed aristocracy and clergy. Khomeini calls the referendum illegitimate and anti-Islamic; his public denunciations lead to the June 1963 Khordad uprising and his exile.

sources.name: Marvin Zonis, 'The Political Elite of Iran' (Princeton UP, 1971)
Abbas Milani, 'The Shah' (Palgrave Macmillan, 2011)

events.title: Algiers Accord — Iran-Iraq Border and Shatt al-Arab
events.detail: Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi and Iraqi Vice President Saddam Hussein sign the Algiers Accord brokered by Algerian President Boumediene, settling the Shatt al-Arab (Arvand Rud) waterway dispute with the thalweg (midchannel) line as border. Iran agrees to stop arming Kurdish rebels in Iraq (KDP under Barzani); Iraq agrees to the waterway demarcation. The agreement ends Iranian support for the Kurdish rebellion which collapses immediately. Saddam repudiates the accord in September 1980 as justification for invading Iran.

sources.name: UN Treaty Collection, 'Algiers Accord 1975'
Dilip Hiro, 'The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict' (Routledge, 1991)

events.title: Islamic Revolution — Shah Flees, Khomeini Returns
events.detail: After a year of escalating strikes and mass demonstrations, Mohammad Reza Shah leaves Iran on January 16, 1979, never to return. Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, exiled since 1964, returns to Tehran on February 1 to a crowd of millions — the largest political gathering in world history. On April 1, after a 98.2% referendum vote, Iran declares itself an Islamic Republic. The revolution fundamentally changes the Middle East's power structure, ending 37 years of US-allied monarchical rule and creating the world's first modern Islamic theocracy governed under Khomeini's doctrine of velayat-e faqih.

sources.name: IRNA — Islamic Republic founding declaration
Ervand Abrahamian, 'Khomeinism' (University of California Press, 1993)
CIA, 'Iran: Prospects for the Revolution' (declassified 1979)

events.title: US Embassy Hostage Crisis — 444 Days
events.detail: On November 4, 1979, Iranian students calling themselves 'Followers of the Imam's Line' storm the US Embassy in Tehran and take 66 American diplomats and staff hostage, demanding the return of the Shah (in the US for cancer treatment). Khomeini endorses the takeover. The crisis lasts 444 days, destroys the Carter presidency, fails with the April 1980 Desert One rescue mission (8 US soldiers killed), and ends January 20, 1981 — the moment Reagan is inaugurated — after Algerian mediation. The Algiers Accords (Iran-US Claims Tribunal) resolve financial claims to this day.

sources.name: Jimmy Carter Presidential Library — official records
David Farber, 'Taken Hostage' (Princeton UP, 2005)
IRNA — Iranian account of embassy takeover

events.title: Iran-Iraq War Begins — Saddam Invades
events.detail: Iraqi President Saddam Hussein launches a full-scale invasion of Iran on September 22, 1980, repudiating the 1975 Algiers Accord and crossing the Shatt al-Arab. Iraqi forces capture Khorramshahr after street-by-street fighting ('City of Blood') and besiege Abadan. Saddam calculates the revolutionary chaos has left Iran militarily weak. The conflict — the longest conventional war of the 20th century — lasts eight years. Iran fights back with mass mobilization (Basij volunteer forces), eventually recapturing Khorramshahr (May 1982). International powers including the US and Western Europe covertly support Iraq with intelligence and weapons.

sources.name: UN Security Council Resolution 479 (1980)
Dilip Hiro, 'The Longest War' (Routledge, 1991)
IRNA — Iranian historical record of the war

events.title: Iraq Uses Chemical Weapons Against Iranian Forces
events.detail: Iraq begins systematic use of chemical weapons (mustard gas, tabun nerve agent) against Iranian forces from 1983, eventually expanding to Halabja (Kurdish civilians, March 1988). The US provides intelligence to Iraq throughout, despite internal CIA knowledge of chemical weapons use. Over 100,000 Iranians are exposed to chemical agents; tens of thousands of veterans suffer long-term health consequences. The Iranian government estimates 53,000 casualties from chemical weapons. International condemnation is muted, given the West's support for Iraq as a counter to revolutionary Iran.

sources.name: Shane Harris & Matthew Aid, 'CIA Files Prove America Helped Saddam Use Chemical Weapons' (Foreign Policy, 2013)
Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW)
Iran Chemical Weapons Victims Foundation (Bonyadeh Janbazane Shahid Chemiyay)

events.title: Iran-Contra Affair — US Secretly Arms Iran
events.detail: A Reagan administration scandal reveals that the US secretly sold anti-tank and anti-aircraft missiles to Iran (despite an arms embargo), diverting proceeds to fund Nicaraguan Contra rebels in violation of the Boland Amendment. CIA Director William Casey and National Security Council staffer Oliver North are central figures. The US had also been arming Iraq — simultaneously arming both sides of the war. The affair badly damages Reagan's presidency and reveals the contradictions of US Middle East policy during the Cold War.

sources.name: Tower Commission Report (1987)
Lawrence Walsh, 'Iran-Contra: The Final Report' (1994)

events.title: Iran Accepts UN Resolution 598 — War Ends
events.detail: Ayatollah Khomeini accepts UN Security Council Resolution 598 calling for a ceasefire in July 1988, famously declaring it was 'more deadly than taking poison.' Iran had suffered reversals including the downing of Iran Air Flight 655 by the USS Vincennes (July 3, killing 290 civilians) and Iraqi recapture of the Faw Peninsula. The war ends in a status quo ante bellum — no territory changes hands. Iranian casualties: 188,000–300,000 military killed, up to 600,000 total (official figures are state secrets). The ceasefire takes effect August 20, 1988.

sources.name: UN Security Council Resolution 598 (1987)
Dilip Hiro, 'The Longest War' (Routledge, 1991)

events.title: 1988 Political Prisoner Massacres — Thousands Executed
events.detail: Following a fatwa by Ayatollah Khomeini, special 'Death Commissions' conduct rapid trials of political prisoners and execute thousands — mainly Mujahedin-e Khalq (MEK/MKO) and Tudeh communist party members — over several weeks in the summer and fall of 1988. Estimates range from 1,400 (official denials) to 30,000 (MEK claims), with Amnesty International and HRW estimating 4,500–5,000 confirmed deaths. Ebrahim Raisi served on the Tehran Death Commission and later became President (2021). The executions remain a taboo subject in Iran; no official accounting has occurred.

sources.name: Amnesty International, 'Blood-Soaked Secrets' (2018)
Human Rights Watch, '1988 Massacre Investigation' (2020)
Grand Ayatollah Montazeri's memoir describing the fatwa (published posthumously 2016)

events.title: Khomeini Dies — Khamenei Becomes Supreme Leader
events.detail: Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini dies on June 3, 1989, triggering an outpouring of grief estimated at 10 million people in Tehran for the funeral procession. The Assembly of Experts hastily appoints Ali Khamenei — a mid-ranking cleric — as the new Supreme Leader; hours later his rank is retroactively elevated to Grand Ayatollah. Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani becomes President. The constitutional revision of 1989 abolishes the post of Prime Minister, strengthening the Presidency while leaving ultimate power with the Supreme Leader — a configuration that continues to the present day.

sources.name: Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1989 revision)
Wilfried Buchta, 'Who Rules Iran?' (Washington Institute for Near East Policy, 2000)

events.title: Khatami Elected — Political Reform and Civil Society
events.detail: Mohammad Khatami wins a landslide presidential election with 70% of the vote, defeating the establishment candidate. His platform of civil society, rule of law, and 'dialogue of civilizations' inspires the reform movement. Under Khatami, newspapers multiply, student activism flourishes, and Iran engages diplomatically with Europe. Conservative hardliners — using the judiciary, Guardian Council, and Revolutionary Guards — systematically kill, imprison, and disqualify reformists. The 1999 student protests are crushed. Khatami's failure to overcome structural resistance leads to reformist disillusionment.

sources.name: Reuters, 'Iran's Khatami wins 70 percent of presidential vote' (May 1997)
Azar Nafisi, 'Reading Lolita in Tehran' (Random House, 2003)

events.title: Natanz Nuclear Facility Revealed — Nuclear Crisis Begins
events.detail: The Iranian opposition group National Council of Resistance of Iran (NCRI) publicly reveals on August 14, 2002 that Iran has been secretly constructing an underground uranium enrichment facility at Natanz and a heavy-water production plant at Arak — both undeclared to the IAEA. The revelation triggers an international crisis. IAEA Director Mohamed ElBaradei confirms the facilities in February 2003. Iran claims the program is purely civilian; the US, EU, and Israel accuse Iran of pursuing nuclear weapons capability. The nuclear standoff, now in its third decade, begins here.

sources.name: IAEA Director General Report GOV/2003/40 (June 2003)
NCRI press conference, Washington DC (August 14, 2002)

events.title: Ahmadinejad Elected — Confrontational Nuclear Posture
events.detail: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, a conservative hardliner and former IRGC commander, wins the presidential election, defeating Rafsanjani. He restarts uranium enrichment at Isfahan (suspended since 2003) and later Natanz. His inflammatory speeches (questioning the Holocaust, calling for Israel to be 'wiped off the map' — disputed translation), Holocaust denial conference, and belligerent UN speeches isolate Iran internationally. Under his presidency, Iran faces UN Security Council Chapter VII sanctions (Resolutions 1737, 1747, 1803, 1929) and accelerated its uranium enrichment despite international pressure.

sources.name: UN Security Council Resolution 1929 (2010)
NYT — Ahmadinejad UN speeches coverage (2005–2013)

events.title: Green Movement — Disputed Election, Mass Protests
events.detail: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad is declared winner of the June 12, 2009 presidential election with 63% of the vote — a result immediately disputed by reformist candidates Mir Hossein Mousavi and Mehdi Karroubi. Millions take to Tehran's streets chanting 'Where is my vote?' in what becomes the Green Movement. The IRGC and Basij violently suppress protests; dozens are killed (government admits 36, opposition claims hundreds), thousands arrested, and rape and torture in detention facilities is documented. Ayatollah Khamenei endorses the result. The movement is crushed within weeks but its memory drives subsequent protests.

sources.name: Amnesty International, 'Iran: Election Contested, Repression Compounded' (2009)
Human Rights Watch, 'Iran: Stop Postelection Violence' (2009)
IRNA — Official election results announcement (June 13, 2009)

events.title: Stuxnet Cyberweapon Destroys Iranian Centrifuges
events.detail: A sophisticated computer worm called Stuxnet — later confirmed by the NYT (2012) to be a joint US-Israeli cyberweapon — infects Iran's nuclear facilities and destroys approximately 1,000 IR-1 centrifuges at Natanz by causing them to spin at destructive speeds while reporting normal operation. The operation (codenamed Olympic Games, authorized by Bush and expanded by Obama) is the first known case of a cyberweapon causing physical destruction to industrial infrastructure. Iran publicly denies significant damage but the IAEA reports a setback in Iran's enrichment capacity.

sources.name: David Sanger, 'Confront and Conceal' (Crown, 2012) — confirms US-Israel role
IAEA, 'Implementation of the NPT Safeguards Agreement' (GOV/2010/10)

events.title: Rouhani Elected — Nuclear Negotiations Accelerate
events.detail: Hassan Rouhani, a moderate cleric and nuclear negotiator, wins the 2013 presidential election with 50.7% — avoiding a runoff. He campaigns on economic recovery through nuclear deal and engagement with the West. His first UN speech sees him shake hands with President Obama's Secretary of State Kerry — the highest-level US-Iran contact since 1979. Foreign Minister Javad Zarif leads negotiations with the P5+1 (US, UK, France, Russia, China + Germany). An interim deal (JPOA) is signed November 2013, freezing enrichment in exchange for partial sanctions relief.

sources.name: Reuters, 'Rouhani wins Iran election' (June 2013)
Joint Plan of Action (JPOA) text (November 24, 2013)

events.title: JCPOA Signed — Historic Nuclear Deal
events.detail: After 20 months of negotiations, Iran and the P5+1 powers sign the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) in Vienna on July 14, 2015. Iran agrees to limit enrichment to 3.67%, cap centrifuge numbers, convert Fordow to a research center, redesign Arak, and accept unprecedented IAEA monitoring. In exchange, all nuclear-related UN Security Council sanctions and US/EU secondary sanctions are lifted. Iran receives access to $100 billion in frozen assets. IAEA certifies Implementation Day (January 16, 2016). The deal is widely hailed as a diplomatic achievement; hardliners on all sides oppose it.

sources.name: JCPOA full text (UN Document A/RES/70/47, 2015)
IAEA Implementation Day certification (January 16, 2016)
NYT, 'Iran Reaches Nuclear Accord With World Powers' (July 14, 2015)

events.title: Trump Withdraws from JCPOA — 'Maximum Pressure' Begins
events.detail: President Trump withdraws the United States from the JCPOA on May 8, 2018, calling it 'a horrible one-sided deal.' He reimposed sweeping sanctions, including on Iran's oil, banking, shipping, and auto sectors — eventually sanctioning over 1,500 Iranian entities. Iran begins incrementally breaching JCPOA limits (May 2019 onward), raising enrichment from 3.67% to 20%, then 60%, and installing advanced centrifuges. The US kills over $40 billion in annual Iranian oil revenues. The European signatories remain in the JCPOA but cannot protect companies from US secondary sanctions.

sources.name: White House statement on JCPOA withdrawal (May 8, 2018)
US Treasury OFAC sanctions designations (2018–2020)

events.title: Qasem Soleimani Killed in US Drone Strike — Regional Crisis
events.detail: IRGC Quds Force commander Qasem Soleimani — arguably the second most powerful figure in Iran — is killed by a US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport on January 3, 2020, along with Iraqi Hashd al-Sha'bi commander Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis. Trump authorizes the strike, citing an 'imminent threat.' Iran retaliates with a January 8 ballistic missile attack on the Al-Asad airbase in Iraq (no US deaths, but 110 soldiers suffer traumatic brain injuries). Days later, the IRGC accidentally shoots down Ukrainian Airlines Flight 752 killing 176 people (January 8, 2020).

sources.name: US DoD statement on Soleimani strike (January 3, 2020)
IRNA — Iranian government statement and funeral coverage (January 2020)
Reuters, 'Iran missiles hit US targets in Iraq' (January 8, 2020)

events.title: Iran Shoots Down Ukrainian Airlines PS752 — 176 Killed
events.detail: Ukraine International Airlines Flight 752, a Boeing 737-800, is shot down by an IRGC surface-to-air missile shortly after takeoff from Tehran on January 8, 2020 — hours after Iran's missile attack on Iraq. All 176 passengers and crew die, including 57 Canadian citizens and 82 Iranians. The IRGC initially denies responsibility; after three days of international pressure and viral video evidence, Iran admits the 'unintentional' shootdown blaming human error during a high-alert period. The incident triggers massive street protests in Iran calling for the Supreme Leader's resignation.

sources.name: Iranian Civil Aviation Organization official statement (January 11, 2020)
Canadian government PS752 investigation (2020)
ICAO formal investigation report (2021)

events.title: Mahsa Amini Killed — 'Woman, Life, Freedom' Uprising
events.detail: 22-year-old Kurdish-Iranian woman Mahsa (Zhina) Amini dies on September 16, 2022, three days after being arrested by Iran's morality police for allegedly wearing her hijab improperly. State autopsy denies abuse; her family disputes this. Her death triggers the largest protests since the 1979 revolution, spreading to all 31 provinces and over 150 cities. The 'Woman, Life, Freedom' (Zan, Zendegi, Azadi) movement is led by young women burning headscarves. Iran's security forces kill at least 516 people (IHR count), arrest over 19,000, and execute at least 4 protesters. Protests subside by early 2023 but social resistance continues.

sources.name: Iran Human Rights (IHR) protest death toll report (2023)
Amnesty International, 'Iran: Wave of Arrests and Executions' (2023)
IRNA — Iranian government statement on protests (September 2022)

events.title: Saudi-Iran Normalization — China Brokers Historic Deal
events.detail: China brokers a historic agreement (March 10, 2023) for Saudi Arabia and Iran to restore diplomatic relations after a 7-year break (following the 2016 Saudi execution of Shia cleric Sheikh Nimr al-Nimr). Both countries reopen embassies by May 2023. The deal, negotiated in secret in Beijing, is China's most significant Middle East diplomatic intervention, signaling Beijing's growing regional role and the limits of US influence. Saudi Arabia and Iran agree to respect sovereignty and non-interference — though Iranian proxy activity continues in Yemen, Iraq, Lebanon, and Gaza.

sources.name: Joint trilateral statement, Beijing (March 10, 2023)
Reuters, 'Saudi Arabia and Iran restore ties in China-brokered deal' (March 10, 2023)

events.title: Iran Directly Attacks Israel for First Time — Operation True Promise
events.detail: Iran launches 'Operation True Promise' on April 13–14, 2024 — its first-ever direct military attack on Israeli territory — in retaliation for Israel's bombing of the Iranian consulate in Damascus (April 1), killing 16 including IRGC Brigadier General Mohammad Reza Zahedi. Iran fires approximately 170 drones, 30 cruise missiles, and 120 ballistic missiles at Israel. Israel, US, UK, France, and Jordan intercept ~99% of projectiles; minor damage to Nevatim Air Base. Iran calls the operation measured and complete. Israel retaliates with limited strikes on Isfahan air defenses (April 19). This marks the first direct Iran-Israel military exchange.

sources.name: IRGC statement, 'Operation True Promise' (April 14, 2024)
IDF spokesperson statement on interceptions (April 14, 2024)
Reuters, 'Iran launches drone and missile attack on Israel' (April 14, 2024)

events.title: President Raisi Dies in Helicopter Crash
events.detail: President Ebrahim Raisi and Foreign Minister Hossein Amir-Abdollahian die in a helicopter crash in foggy mountains near the Azerbaijan border on May 19, 2024. The Russian-made Mi-17 crashes while returning from inaugurating a dam on the Aras River with Azerbaijani President Aliyev. Raisi, 63, had been considered a likely candidate to succeed Khamenei as Supreme Leader. First Vice President Mohammad Mokhber serves as acting president. The incident triggers an early presidential election in June 2024.

sources.name: IRNA — Official statement on Raisi death (May 20, 2024)
Reuters, 'Iran President Raisi confirmed dead in helicopter crash' (May 20, 2024)

events.title: Masoud Pezeshkian Elected — Reformist Wins Presidency
events.detail: Masoud Pezeshkian, a heart surgeon and reformist MP from Tabriz, wins the June/July 2024 presidential election in a runoff against hardliner Saeed Jalili, receiving 16.4 million votes (54.8%). His victory surprises many analysts given the Guardian Council's usual vetting of reformists. Pezeshkian campaigns on re-engaging with the West on nuclear negotiations, easing social restrictions, and economic revival. He appoints Mohammad Javad Zarif as strategic advisor and retains a pragmatic foreign policy team. Supreme Leader Khamenei limits his room for maneuver on structural issues.

sources.name: Iranian Interior Ministry — official election results (July 6, 2024)
Reuters, 'Iran reformist Pezeshkian wins presidential election' (July 6, 2024)

events.title: Nuclear Negotiations Resume Under Pezeshkian Presidency
events.detail: Under President Pezeshkian, Iran resumes indirect talks with the United States facilitated by Oman, while direct EU-Iran technical discussions continue in Geneva. Iran's uranium stockpile of 60%-enriched material reaches approximately 182 kg by early 2025 (sufficient for 3–4 weapons if further enriched to 90%). Trump's return to the US presidency (January 2025) creates uncertainty; his administration signals both willingness to deal and threats of military strikes. Iran executes several individuals accused of spying for Israel and the US (2025). The nuclear standoff continues with no breakthrough as of April 2026.

sources.name: IAEA Director General Report, 'Iran Nuclear' (GOV/2025/12)
Reuters, 'Iran uranium stockpile at near-weapons-grade nears critical threshold' (2025)
IRNA — Iranian diplomatic statements (2025–2026)
